rootCause: Computer name entered in mstsc does not match AAD device hostnames attribute. E.g. using FQDN device_1.contoso.com when AAD device name is short name device_1.
solution: 1. Use exact AAD device name (short name) in mstsc. 2. Or add Primary DNS Suffix on target machine (Advanced System Settings > Computer Name > Change > More) to register FQDN in AAD hostnames. 3. Or add A record in client HOSTS file pointing to correct device name. 4. Check if DNS Client PrimaryDnsSuffix GPO/MDM is set incorrectly - it takes precedence.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/RDS AAD Auth Troubleshooting Guide

symptom: Repeated Windows login prompt when RDP to HAADJ target machine via RDS AAD Auth - cannot sign in
rootCause: Kerberos Server Object missing for user on-prem domain, or domain controller unreachable from target machine. On-prem TGT required for HAADJ login cannot be obtained.
solution: 1. Verify Kerberos Server Object exists: run Set-AzureAdKerberosServer for each on-prem domain. 2. Check network connectivity from target to DC. 3. Capture network trace (Netmon.etl) and Kerberos trace (Kerberos.etl) from target machine Auth logs to confirm TGT exchange.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/RDS AAD Auth Troubleshooting Guide

symptom: Repeated AAD login prompt (not Windows prompt) when RDP to HAADJ target via RDS AAD Auth - KDC_ERR_TGT_REVOKED in network trace
rootCause: User account is member of Domain Admins group or local admin group. By design, domain admin/local admin connections are denied when using RDS AAD Auth.
solution: If customer needs Domain Admin login, change default Password Replication Policy for AzureADKerberos computer object per MS docs: configure-single-sign-on#allow-active-directory-domain-administrator-accounts-to-connect.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/RDS AAD Auth Troubleshooting Guide

symptom: Run as Different User fails with username or password is incorrect on AVD AADJ device (works on physical AADJ device)
rootCause: Run-as-different-user triggers non-interactive auth for resource app Azure Windows VM Sign-In. CA policy requiring MFA blocks non-interactive auth. Also, missing RBAC roles (VM User Login/VM Administrator Login) causes same error.
solution: 1. Assign RBAC roles VM User Login or VM Administrator Login to the run-as user. 2. Check AAD Sign-in logs for CA policy blocking Azure Windows VM Sign-in resource. 3. Exclude Azure Windows VM Sign-in app from MFA-requiring CA policy.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/RDS AAD Auth Troubleshooting Guide

symptom: SSO not working when connecting to new AVD session host - fresh AAD auth + consent prompt Allow remote desktop connection required each time
rootCause: By design: each new session host requires fresh auth and consent. AAD cache limit is 15 hosts for 30 days.
solution: Configure target device groups per MS docs: configure-single-sign-on#configure-the-target-device-groups to suppress consent prompt and enable SSO across session hosts.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/RDS AAD Auth Troubleshooting Guide

symptom: AADSTS9002341 'User is required to permit SSO' error in WAM; apps fail silent auth after DMA compliance enforcement
rootCause: Digital Markets Act (DMA) compliance in Windows requires user consent for SSO. Silent WAM calls now fail, exposing latent issues (AV blocking WAM plugin, app not integrated with WAM UI flow)
solution: By design - NEVER disable WAM or recommend ADAL. If calling app doesn't support WAM UI (RequestTokenAsync), escalate to app owner. If DMA block exposes AV issues, follow AV/AppLocker exclusion steps. Never modify IntegratedServicesRegionPolicySet.json
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Troubleshooting WAM related SSO issues

symptom: WAM auth fails with 0x80070520 'A specified logon session does not exist'; RefreshTokenRequest error
rootCause: User SID changed (e.g. domain migration) but WAM profile data still cached with old SID
solution: Update Windows (RS5: KB4541331, 19H1: KB4541335). For RS4 and below: delete files from %LOCALAPPDATA%\Packages\Microsoft.AAD.BrokerPlugin_cw5n1h2txyewy\AC\TokenBroker\Accounts and CloudExperienceHost equivalent, then reboot
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Troubleshooting WAM related SSO issues

symptom: Sign-in prompt loop with AADSTS135011 'Device used during authentication is disabled'; AADSTS70002 credential validation error
rootCause: Device object disabled in Azure AD by admin, policy, or security concern
solution: Enterprise admin re-enables the device in Azure AD portal (Manage device identities). Verify device not accidentally disabled by stale device cleanup policy
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Troubleshooting WAM related SSO issues

symptom: WAM auth fails with 0x8009002C 'The specified data could not be decrypted'; DPAPI failure at AuthorizationClient::Deserialize
rootCause: DPAPI corruption on device - credential blobs encrypted with DPAPI keys that are now inaccessible
solution: Clear WAM state: stop TokenBroker service (Set-Service Disabled + Stop-Service), kill BrokerPlugin.exe, delete TokenBroker\Accounts files + settings.dat, rename DefaultAccount registry key, restart TokenBroker service. If WPJ user, must re-join device
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Troubleshooting WAM related SSO issues

symptom: App fails to get WAM token with 0x42b/1067 ERROR_PROCESS_ABORTED or 0x80080300 BT_E_SPURIOUS_ACTIVATION; AV/AppLocker blocks Microsoft.AAD.BrokerPlugin
rootCause: Security software (antivirus, AppLocker, or endpoint protection) blocks WAM plugin execution or injects code causing crashes
solution: Create exclusions for: binaries (Microsoft.AAD.BrokerPlugin.exe, backgroundTaskHost.exe) and directories (%windir%\SystemApps\Microsoft.AAD.BrokerPlugin_*, %localappdata%\Packages\Microsoft.AAD.BrokerPlugin_*, same for CloudExperienceHost). Check ProcMon for 3rd-party DLLs loaded into BrokerPlugin process. See aka.ms docs for full exclusion list
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Troubleshooting WAM related SSO issues

symptom: PRT acquisition fails with STATUS_LOGON_FAILURE (0xc000006d) or STATUS_WRONG_PASSWORD (0xc000006a) on Azure AD Joined or Hybrid Joined Windows device
rootCause: Device unable to connect to AAD authentication service, or received HTTP 400 error from AAD auth service or WS-Trust endpoint. Outbound proxy may block computer account authentication.
solution: 1) Ensure computer account can discover and silently authenticate to outbound proxy. 2) Check Events 1081/1088 in AAD Operational logs for server error code. 3) Check Event 1022 in AAD Analytic logs for the URL being accessed. 4) For federated auth, verify WS-Trust endpoint is reachable.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Troubleshooting Windows 10 Azure AD Joined Authentication

symptom: PRT acquisition fails with STATUS_NO_SUCH_LOGON_SESSION (0xc000005f) — user realm discovery failed
rootCause: AAD authentication service unable to find the user's domain because the UPN domain is not added as a custom domain in AAD, or the on-premises domain is non-routable (e.g. contoso.local).
solution: 1) Add the domain of the user's UPN as a custom domain in AAD. 2) If on-prem domain is non-routable, configure Alternate Login ID (AltID). 3) Check Event 1144 in AAD Analytic logs for the UPN provided.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Troubleshooting Windows 10 Azure AD Joined Authentication

symptom: PRT acquisition fails with AADSTS50155: Device authentication failed on Azure AD Joined or Hybrid Joined device
rootCause: AAD is unable to authenticate the device to issue a PRT. The device object may have been deleted or disabled in the Azure portal.
solution: 1) Confirm device has not been deleted or disabled in Azure portal (Devices blade). 2) Re-register the device based on join type: for AADJ — re-join; for Hybrid AADJ — re-sync via Azure AD Connect or re-join domain. See Microsoft docs for device re-registration steps.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Troubleshooting Windows 10 Azure AD Joined Authentication

symptom: PRT acquisition fails with AADSTS50126: Error validating credentials due to invalid username or password on Hybrid Joined device after password change
rootCause: If tenant has Password Hash Sync enabled and device is Hybrid Joined, the new password may not have synced to AAD yet after a recent password change. AAD still validates against the old password hash.
solution: Wait for the AAD Connect sync cycle to complete (default 30 min) to acquire a fresh PRT with the new credentials. Can force sync via Start-ADSyncSyncCycle -PolicyType Delta on the AAD Connect server.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Troubleshooting Windows 10 Azure AD Joined Authentication

symptom: PRT acquisition fails with AAD_CLOUDAP_E_WSTRUST_SAML_TOKENS_ARE_EMPTY (0xc00484c1) on federated domain — WS-Trust SAML tokens empty
rootCause: WS-Trust endpoint returned an error or empty SAML assertion. Network proxy may be interfering with WS-Trust response, or federation service (ADFS) is misconfigured.
solution: 1) Ensure network proxy is not modifying WS-Trust response. 2) Check Event 1088 in AAD Operational logs for server error code and description from WS-Trust endpoint. 3) Verify ADFS WS-Trust endpoints are enabled and reachable (windowstransport, usernamemixed).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Troubleshooting Windows 10 Azure AD Joined Authentication

symptom: WebView2 WAM sign-in: IdP login page shows blank or broken UI in mini-browser after enabling WebView2Integration registry key on Windows 24H2/25H2
rootCause: Third-party IdP relies on legacy EdgeHTML behaviors or user-agent detection (e.g. Edge/18.x) that does not recognize Chromium-based WebView2 engine
solution: IdP must update login page for Chromium compatibility: use capability-based detection instead of user-agent checks. Quick test: if page fails in Edge browser, it will fail in WebView2.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/WebView2 integration in Entra ID WAM

symptom: WebView2 integration not enabled in WAM despite setting WebView2Integration registry key to 1
rootCause: Device not patched to min build (26100.7462 or 26200.7462), registry key path/value incorrect, or device not rebooted
solution: 1) Verify Win 24H2 26100.7462+ or 25H2 26200.7462+. 2) Confirm HKLM Software Policies Microsoft Windows AAD WebView2Integration = DWORD 1. 3) Reboot. Verify via Task Manager: multiple processes under Work or School account.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/WebView2 integration in Entra ID WAM

symptom: WebView2 embedded browser in WAM closes immediately after opening, shows blank window, or cannot connect to internet
rootCause: Third-party security software (antivirus/DLP) injecting DLLs into msedgewebview2.exe or Microsoft.AAD.BrokerPlugin.exe
solution: Use Procmon to check for external DLLs in BrokerPlugin.exe or msedgewebview2.exe child processes. Exclude msedgewebview2.exe from antivirus/DLP scanning.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/WebView2 integration in Entra ID WAM

symptom: WebView2 WAM auth fails: browser crashes after customer hardened System32 permissions
rootCause: ALL APPLICATION PACKAGES permissions removed from System32 folder causing integrity level mismatch for WebView2 runtime
solution: Restore default ALL APPLICATION PACKAGES permissions on C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft\EdgeWebView\Application AND C:\Windows\System32\Microsoft-Edge-WebView.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/WebView2 integration in Entra ID WAM

symptom: WebView2 WAM mini-browser no internet - auth flow fails while Edge browser works fine
rootCause: Firewall blocking msedgewebview2.exe outbound connections - WebView2 uses Chromium networking stack separate from Windows networking
solution: Remove msedgewebview2.exe from firewall blocked applications list. WebView2 is Chromium-based with different proxy/TLS behavior than legacy EdgeHTML - verify proxy and TLS compatibility separately.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/WebView2 integration in Entra ID WAM

symptom: IDP login page renders in Edge browser but fails/blank in WAM WebView2 mini-browser (BrokerPlugin running from System32)
rootCause: Problem with System WebView2 runtime files in System32\Microsoft-Edge-Webview - sandboxed system runtime behaves differently than Evergreen runtime
solution: Engage Windows Platforms support for System runtime issues. Escalation: ICM via aka.ms/wamhot, Scenario: Cloud Identity AuthN Client Team (Windows). Try reproducing with Evergreen runtime for comparison.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/WebView2 integration in Entra ID WAM

symptom: Windows 10 Pro does not auto-upgrade to Enterprise via Subscription Activation on AAD joined / Hybrid AAD joined device
rootCause: User does not have WIN10_PRO_ENT_SUB service plan (ID: 21b439ba-a0ca-424f-a6cc-52f954a5b111) assigned
solution: Verify in ASC > Tenant Explorer > User blade > Licenses > Assigned Plans, filter by Service Plan ID 21b439ba-a0ca-424f-a6cc-52f954a5b111. If missing, assign Windows 10 Enterprise E3/E5 license to user.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Windows 10 Subscription Activation

symptom: Windows 10 Subscription Activation fails - LicenseAcquisition scheduled task errors, AAD event log shows token acquisition failure for onestore.microsoft.com
rootCause: WAM plugin cannot silently acquire access token for https://onestore.microsoft.com using PRT, or licensing.mp.microsoft.com rejects the request. Common causes: PRT not available, proxy/firewall blocking endpoints, stale device state.
solution: Collect system-context Fiddler trace using PsExec -s (LicenseAcquisition runs as SYSTEM). Check AAD Operational event logs: Get-WinEvent -LogName 'Microsoft-Windows-AAD/Operational' | ?{$_.message -like '*onestore.microsoft.com*'}. Check Client-Licensing event logs at Applications and Service Logs > Windows > Client-Licensing > Admin. Verify PRT status with dsregcmd /status.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Windows 10 Subscription Activation

symptom: RDP sign-in to Windows 365 AADJ Cloud PC fails when connecting from Windows desktop client (msrdc.exe), PKU2U authentication error
rootCause: Local device is not AADJ/HAADJ/Registered in the same tenant as the Cloud PC, or PKU2U protocol is disabled by Security Baseline group policy
solution: Ensure local device is Azure AD Joined, Hybrid Azure AD Joined, or Registered in same tenant as Cloud PC. If Security Baseline policies are deployed to Cloud PCs, re-enable PKU2U protocol in group policy. Alternative: use web client, Android, macOS, or iOS client which support username/password without local device state requirement.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Windows 365 AADJ Cloud PC

symptom: RDP sign-in to Windows 365 Cloud PC fails for user with Per-User MFA enabled
rootCause: Per-User MFA is not supported for Windows 365 Cloud PC RDP sign-in
solution: Switch from Per-User MFA to Conditional Access based MFA. Disable Per-User MFA for the affected user and configure CA policy with MFA requirement instead.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Windows 365 AADJ Cloud PC

symptom: Customer reports Azure AD Join failure or provisioning issue on device with displayname starting with 'CPC-' (Windows 365 Cloud PC)
rootCause: Windows 365 Cloud PCs use a unique 'zero touch' AADJ process, not standard Entra ID AADJ. AADJ failures on W365 Cloud PCs are provisioning failures owned by W365 team.
solution: Identify Cloud PC: displayname starts with 'CPC-', Device Model in Intune shows 'Cloud PC Enterprise'. Transfer case to W365 team: Business → SAP 'Windows 365\Windows 365\Windows 365 Business Edition\Provisioning\Provisioning Failure'; Enterprise → SAP 'Windows 365\Windows 365\Windows 365 Enterprise Edition\Provisioning\Provisioning Failure'.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Windows 365 AADJ Cloud PC

symptom: LAPS password not updated to Azure AD. Event ID 10059 with error Local admin password solution is not enabled for this tenant. Reset-LapsPassword fails with hr:0x80070190
rootCause: LAPS feature not enabled in Azure AD tenant Device settings (disabled by default)
solution: Navigate to Azure AD > Devices > Device settings, toggle Enable Azure AD Local Administrator Password Solution (LAPS) to Yes. Can also enable via Graph API PUT https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/policies/deviceRegistrationPolicy
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Windows Local Administrator Password Solution Backed up to Azure AD

symptom: LAPS password update fails with HTTP 400 Event 10059: The device deviceId in tenantId could not be found
rootCause: Device was removed or deleted from the Azure AD tenant
solution: Check device registration status with dsregcmd /status. Re-register the device to Azure AD if needed.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Windows Local Administrator Password Solution Backed up to Azure AD

symptom: LAPS password update fails with HTTP 403 Event 10059: The specified request is not allowed for tenant
rootCause: Device has been disabled in Azure AD
solution: Re-enable the device in Azure AD portal > Devices. Check device status with dsregcmd /status.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Windows Local Administrator Password Solution Backed up to Azure AD

symptom: LAPS Azure discovery fails. Event ID 10025 Azure discovery failed with error code 0x80072EE7 ERROR_ADAL_INTERNET_NAME_NOT_RESOLVED
rootCause: Network connectivity issue - enterpriseregistration.windows.net endpoint is unreachable from the client device
solution: Verify network connectivity: run curl https://enterpriseregistration.windows.net/ -D - from admin command prompt. Fix firewall/proxy rules to allow access.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Windows Local Administrator Password Solution Backed up to Azure AD

symptom: Event ID 10032 - LAPS fails to authenticate to Azure using device identity
rootCause: Device credential/certificate issue preventing authentication to Azure AD Device Registration Service
solution: Check device registration status with dsregcmd /status. Verify device certificate is valid. Re-register device if certificate is expired or missing. Run Reset-LapsPassword -Verbose -Debug for details.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Windows Local Administrator Password Solution Backed up to Azure AD
Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Hybrid Join/Pending Devices - Microsoft Entra Hybrid Join
rootCause: Device cannot connect to Azure AD device registration service endpoints or cannot reach on-premises AD domain controller to discover SCP object. Common causes: outbound proxy blocking, WPAD not configured, network filtering
solution: Verify device can communicate with Microsoft registration endpoints (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/devices/how-to-hybrid-join#network-connectivity-requirements) under system account. For authenticated proxy, ensure machine context auth works. Implement WPAD or configure WinHTTP proxy via GPO (Windows 10 1709+). For managed environments, device creates UserCertificate for AAD Connect sync; for federated environments, device needs WIA with IDP.
symptom: Hybrid Entra ID Joined device shows Pending registration state in Entra ID portal; device cannot complete registration process

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Hybrid Join/Pending Devices - Microsoft Entra Hybrid Join
rootCause: Device was previously registered then removed from Entra ID. Causes: (1) OU moved out of sync scope then back - device fails re-registration, (2) admin deleted stale devices, (3) VDI golden image unjoin cascaded to all cloned devices via Device Recovery LeaveThenJoin
solution: Run dsregcmd /leave from elevated CMD -> wait for device to disappear from Entra portal (or manually delete) -> reboot machine and sign in with DC line of sight -> for managed domains: run Start-ADSyncSyncCycle -PolicyType Delta in PowerShell then dsregcmd /join; for federated environments: run dsregcmd /join directly (no need to wait for sync cycle)
symptom: Hybrid Entra ID Joined device reverts to Pending state after being moved between OUs or after admin deletes/disables device in Entra ID; device thinks it is still HAADJ locally and will not re-trigger join

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Hybrid Join/Device registration_Troubleshooting Windows 10 Automatic Device Registration
rootCause: TPM version 1.2 is FIPS compliant and Azure device registration uses non-FIPS compliant TPM operations. FIPS Compliant TPM 1.2 is not supported. Does not apply to TPM 2.0.
symptom: Windows 10 Hybrid Join fails with error 0x80280036 - TPM 1.2 FIPS compliance. Event 502: DsrBeginDeviceJoin failed with error code 0x80280036.
solution: Disable the TPM as a workaround. FIPS compliant TPM 1.2 is not supported for Azure device registration.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Hybrid Join/Device registration_Troubleshooting Windows 10 Automatic Device Registration
rootCause: TPM keys not accessible. Windows OS may have failed to initialize/take ownership of the TPM.
symptom: Windows 10 Hybrid Join fails with error 0x80090016 Keyset does not exist. Device registration unable to save device key.
solution: Clear/reset TPM: Windows Defender Security Center > Device security > Security processor details > Clear TPM. Reboot. Disable BitLocker first if enabled. For managed domains, run delta sync on AAD Connect after.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Hybrid Join/Device registration_Troubleshooting Windows 10 Automatic Device Registration
rootCause: TPM is in lockout state defending against dictionary attacks, preventing access to TPM keys.
symptom: Windows 10 Hybrid Join fails - TPM lockout / dictionary attack defense. Event 1026: TPM defending against dictionary attacks.
solution: 1. Launch TPM.MSC try unlock/reset. 2. Check BIOS TPM lockout settings. 3. Engage hardware vendor. 4. If no BitLocker: Clear TPM via Windows Defender Security Center.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Hybrid Join/Device registration_Troubleshooting Windows 10 Automatic Device Registration
rootCause: Windows 10 machine does not trust the ADFS server certificate. Root certificate missing from trusted root store.
symptom: Windows 10 Hybrid Join fails in federated environment with error 0xcaa82f8f ERROR_ADAL_INTERNET_SECURE_FAILURE. SSL certificate errors.
solution: Add root certificate of ADFS service communication certificate to trusted root store on clients. If self-signed cert, distribute to all clients.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Hybrid Join/Device registration_Troubleshooting Windows 10 Automatic Device Registration
rootCause: usercertificate attribute on on-prem AD computer object is missing, deleted, or not synced to Azure AD.
symptom: Windows 10 Hybrid Join fails in managed domain with error 0x801c03f2 - The public key user certificate is not found on the device object.
solution: dsregcmd /leave (admin). Delete MS-Organization-Access cert from certlm.msc. Restart client. Verify usercertificate populated and replicated. Run delta sync on AAD Connect.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Hybrid Join/Device registration_Troubleshooting Windows 10 Automatic Device Registration
rootCause: Device object not synced to Azure AD or device object was deleted from Azure AD.
symptom: Windows 10 Hybrid Join fails in managed domain with error 0x801c03f2 - The device object by the given id is not found.
solution: Verify usercertificate on on-prem AD computer. Search AAD Connect metaverse. If missing: dsregcmd /leave + delete MS-Organization-Access cert + restart + delta sync. If exists but ID mismatch, file ICM to PG.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Hybrid Join/Device registration_Troubleshooting Windows 10 Automatic Device Registration
rootCause: Device was Workplace Joined first then Hybrid Joined later, or user performed workplace join before Hybrid Join completed.
symptom: Device in dual state - both Hybrid Joined and Workplace Joined. Same device appears twice in AAD portal. Conditional access issues.
solution: Option 1: Upgrade to Win10 RS5+ (auto cleanup). Option 2: Manual dsregcmd /leave + disconnect workplace from Settings. Option 3: WPJCleanUp tool via GPO. Prevention: Set HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\WorkplaceJoin BlockAADWorkplaceJoin=1.

symptom: Entra Hybrid Join using Entra Kerberos fails with DSREG_TOKEN_MISSING_ON_PREM_ID (0x801c0095) - token doesn't contain an on-premises ID
rootCause: Kerberos ticket from on-premises Kerberos authority doesn't contain information required by Microsoft Entra ID. EnableKerbHaadj.exe not run on Windows Server 2025 domain controllers.
solution: On every domain controller running Windows Server 2025 within the AD domain, run EnableKerbHaadj.exe and restart the DC.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Hybrid Join/Entra Hybrid Join using Entra Kerberos

symptom: Entra Hybrid Join using Entra Kerberos fails with SEC_E_NO_AUTHENTICATING_AUTHORITY (0x80090311) - no authority could be contacted for authentication
rootCause: No functional domain controller running Windows Server 2025 can be contacted by the client device during hybrid join.
solution: 1) Install at least one DC running Windows Server 2025 (build 26100.6905+) in the AD domain. 2) Run EnableKerbHaadj.exe on it and restart. 3) Ensure KDC service is running on this DC. 4) Run dcdiag.exe to verify DC is advertising itself. 5) Ensure client has unimpeded network connectivity to the WS2025 DC.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Hybrid Join/Entra Hybrid Join using Entra Kerberos

symptom: Entra Hybrid Join using Entra Kerberos fails with SEC_E_LOGON_DENIED (0x8009030c) / KDC_ERR_NULL_KEY (0x9) - No KerberosKeyInformation Keys found
rootCause: Kerberos key for the Microsoft Entra device registration service principal (AppId 01cb2876-7ebd-4aa4-9cc9-d28bd4d359a9) is not found. The KerberosPolicy:ExchangeForJwt tag is missing from the DRS service principal.
solution: Add the tag 'KerberosPolicy:ExchangeForJwt' to the DRS service principal using Entra PowerShell: Connect-Entra → Get-EntraServicePrincipal -Filter "AppId eq '01cb2876-7ebd-4aa4-9cc9-d28bd4d359a9'" → Add tag → Set-EntraServicePrincipal. Also ensure SPN 'adrs/enterpriseregistration.windows.net' is present.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Hybrid Join/Entra Hybrid Join using Entra Kerberos

symptom: Slow logon (~21 seconds delay) on Hybrid Joined devices. Unjoining with dsregcmd /leave mitigates the issue. CloudAP plugin DoGetToken step causes the delay during PRT acquisition.
rootCause: Device authentication fails during PRT acquisition. Device object was deleted from Entra ID (admin removed from sync scope, deleted from Azure portal intentionally or accidentally). CloudAP plugin retries 4 times with 3-second timeout each on first logon, totaling ~21 seconds delay. This is NOT a network timeout issue - device can reach login.microsoftonline.com but device auth itself fails.
solution: 1) Gather auth trace and check AAD Operational log event ID 1250 for DoGetToken correlation ID and UTC timestamp. 2) Use ASC > Sign-ins > Diagnostics > Troubleshoot a sign-in with the correlation ID to confirm Device Authentication Failed. 3) Search audit logs (last 30 days, premium licensing required) with device name as target to identify who/what deleted the device. 4) If device should remain hybrid joined: run dsregcmd /leave then rejoin to fix registration state. 5) If intentionally removed: run dsregcmd /leave to clean up local hybrid device state. Note: 'Slow login' cases are owned by Directory Services team for LSASS trace analysis (tss -wpr SBSL). If delay is in aadcloudap.dll!DoGetToken, AAD Auth team partners via collaboration task.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Hybrid Join/Slow Logon On Hybrid Joined Devices

symptom: VDI devices share same Device ID as golden/master image - all provisioned VDIs are unhealthy, dsregcmd /status shows same Device ID across VDIs
rootCause: Golden/Master image was accidentally Microsoft Entra Hybrid Joined before being sealed. All VDIs provisioned from it inherit the same Device ID.
solution: Run dsregcmd /leave from Admin Command Prompt on golden/master image as the last step before shutdown/seal. Verify with dsregcmd /status that AzureAdJoined=NO. Each provisioned VDI must go through Hybrid Join independently.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Hybrid Join/Troubleshooting Persistent and Non-Persistent VDI scenarios

symptom: Non-persistent VDI enters Dual Device state (Entra Registered instead of Hybrid Joined) - user blocked by device-based Conditional Access policies requiring Hybrid Join or Compliant device
rootCause: VDI does not complete Hybrid Join registration before user signs in. User opens Office and selects 'Let my organization manage this device', triggering Entra Registration instead. Federated instant join or sync join hasn't completed in time.
solution: Prevention: Deploy registry key HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\WorkplaceJoin BlockAADWorkplaceJoin=dword:00000001. Recovery: Ensure device completes Hybrid Join, user lock/unlock or sign out/in to obtain PRT, clean up Entra Registered objects. For Federated: implement dsregcmd /join in VM boot sequence before user sign-in. DO NOT execute dsregcmd /leave during shutdown/restart.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Hybrid Join/Troubleshooting Persistent and Non-Persistent VDI scenarios

symptom: SSO/PRT broken on VDI after roaming profile migration - authentication issues, token broker failures on virtual desktops
rootCause: Profile migration tools roamed data under %localappdata% that must never leave the device: BrokerPlugin, CloudExperienceHost, TokenBroker, OneAuth, IdentityCache folders and related registry keys (IdentityCRL, AAD, WorkplaceJoin, TokenBroker).
solution: Never roam: %localappdata%\Packages\Microsoft.AAD.BrokerPlugin_cw5n1h2txyewy, Microsoft.Windows.CloudExperienceHost_cw5n1h2txyewy, */AC/TokenBroker, %localappdata%\Microsoft\{TokenBroker,OneAuth,IdentityCache}, and registry keys HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\{IdentityCRL, Windows\CurrentVersion\AAD, Windows NT\CurrentVersion\WorkplaceJoin, Windows NT\CurrentVersion\TokenBroker}. Also do not roam MS-Organization-Access device certificate.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Hybrid Join/Troubleshooting Persistent and Non-Persistent VDI scenarios

symptom: BitLocker Graph API query fails with BadRequest 400: 'Parameter (ocp-client-name) is required but missing from headers' or 'Parameter (ocp-client-version) is required but missing from headers'
rootCause: BitLocker MS Graph API requires custom headers ocp-client-name and ocp-client-version in every request. These are Azure Device Registration Service headers used to track client identity.
solution: Add both headers to the Graph API request: ocp-client-name and ocp-client-version. Any value will work. To find actual client values, enable browser dev tools > Network tab when viewing recovery key in Azure Portal, look for the request ending in $select=key and check Request Headers for ocp-client-* values.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Join/Audit BitLocker Recovery Keys in Azure AD

symptom: End user cannot perform self-service BitLocker recovery key retrieval from myaccount portal
rootCause: User is not the registered owner of the device, OR device is Hybrid Azure AD Joined (HAADJ) which does not support end-user self-service recovery.
solution: If user is not registered owner: contact tenant administrator and provide the BitLocker Key ID. If device is HAADJ: user must contact tenant administrator. Admin can retrieve via Azure Portal (AAD > Devices > device > Show recovery key) or Graph API GET /BitLocker/recoveryKeys/{keyId}?$select=key.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Join/Audit BitLocker Recovery Keys in Azure AD

symptom: Administrator cannot view BitLocker recovery key in Azure AD Portal or via Graph API
rootCause: Administrator does not have one of the required roles: Global administrator, Cloud device administrator, Helpdesk administrator, Security reader, Security administrator, Intune service administrator, or registered owner.
solution: Assign one of the required roles to the administrator. Minimum role: Cloud device administrator or Helpdesk administrator for key retrieval. Note: Feature is limited to Azure AD Joined Windows 10+ devices only.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Join/Audit BitLocker Recovery Keys in Azure AD

symptom: Admin wants to block BitLocker self-service recovery for end users but setting is all-or-none, cannot be applied to groups or individual users
rootCause: Feature design: 'Restrict non-admin users from recovering the BitLocker key(s) for their owned devices' is a tenant-wide toggle with no group-level granularity.
solution: Configure via: 1) Azure AD Portal > Device Settings > toggle 'Restrict non-admin users from recovering BitLocker keys'. 2) Or Graph API: PATCH https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/policies/authorizationPolicy/authorizationPolicy with body {defaultUserRolePermissions:{allowedToReadBitlockerKeysForOwnedDevice:false}}. Requires Policy.ReadWrite.Authorization permission. Allow ~30 min for changes to take effect.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Join/Blocking Bitlocker self-service recovery for all users

symptom: Azure AD Portal 'Users may register their devices with Azure AD' setting is grayed out and cannot be disabled
rootCause: Microsoft Intune application (AppId 0000000a-0000-0000-c000-000000000000) is enabled in the tenant. When Intune is enabled, it takes control of device registration settings and grays out the portal toggle.
solution: If not using Intune: disable Microsoft Intune Enterprise Application (AppId 0000000a-0000-0000-c000-000000000000) via Enterprise Applications UI. For Gov tenants where Enterprise Apps search may not work: use PowerShell: Connect-MgGraph -Scopes Application.ReadWrite.All → Get-MgServicePrincipalByAppId -AppId 0000000a-0000-0000-c000-000000000000 → Update-MgServicePrincipalByAppId -AppId 0000000a-0000-0000-c000-000000000000 -AccountEnabled $false
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Join/Device registration_Troubleshooting Windows 10 Add Work or School Account

symptom: Windows 10 'Add Work or School Account' MDM enrollment fails with 'Something went wrong' error but work account still appears added
rootCause: Known behavior since RS2: MDM enrollment failure no longer prevents work account from being added. User sees error but account connection succeeds without MDM enrollment.
solution: If admin requires MDM for resource access: user should click 'Connect' again and go through the process to re-enroll into MDM. Note: DeviceRegTroubleshooter tool (https://aka.ms/DSRegTool) performs 30+ tests to identify and fix common device registration issues for all join types.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Join/Device registration_Troubleshooting Windows 10 Add Work or School Account

symptom: Unknown SIDs with prefix S-1-12-1- appear in local Administrators group on Entra ID Joined devices - cannot identify which directory objects they represent
rootCause: When a Windows device is joined to Entra ID, specific roles are added to the local administrators group as SIDs referencing directory objects. These Entra ID mapped SIDs use the S-1-12-1- prefix followed by four dash-separated integers.
solution: Use the SID2GUID PowerShell script (https://github.com/ms-robgarcia/SID2GUID) to convert SIDs to GUIDs and vice versa. Then search the GUID in Azure Support Center (ASC) > Tenant Explorer > Directory object to identify the role or user. Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/devices/assign-local-admin
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Join/Mapping Entra ID Joined Device SIDs to Entra ID Objects

symptom: BitLocker recovery key was backed up to Entra ID but device object was deleted - cannot recover the key
rootCause: BitLocker recovery key is linked to the device object in Entra ID. Unlike users or apps, device objects cannot be restored after deletion (no soft delete).
solution: There is no way to recover the BitLocker key if the associated device object is deleted and the key was not backed up elsewhere. Advise customers to maintain secondary backup of recovery keys. For future prevention: use manage-bde -protectors -get c: to document keys before device deletion.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Join/Troubleshooting BitLocker related issue

symptom: Entra ID BitLocker recovery key storage limit (200 keys) reached - new BitLocker keys cannot be backed up, encryption or key backup fails
rootCause: Entra ID has a hardcoded by-design limitation of 200 BitLocker recovery keys per device. Common in environments with frequent reprovisioning or reencryption.
solution: 1) Delete old/unused BitLocker keys: Win11 (22621+) run manage-bde -protectors -delete c: -id {protectorId} then wait 2 hours for Entra sync (or speed up with reg add HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\BitLocker /f /v PersistentRequestTimerOverrideMs /t REG_DWORD /d 10000). Win10 does NOT support this method. 2) Alternative: remove and re-add the device (creates new device object, clears all keys - backup needed first, deletion is NOT reversible).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Join/Troubleshooting BitLocker related issue

symptom: BitLocker key backed up to wrong device entry on dual state device (both Entra Registered and Hybrid Joined exist)
rootCause: Device was registered to Entra ID prior to Hybrid Join. Entra Registered entry not removed, creating dual state. manage-bde may upload key to either HAADJ or WPJ entry unpredictably.
solution: Remove the unused Workplace Joined (Entra Registered) object to resolve dual state. Always suggest customers clean up unused WPJ objects to avoid this scenario.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Join/Troubleshooting BitLocker related issue

symptom: BitLocker key backup to Entra ID fails - event ID 846 in Microsoft-Windows-BitLocker-API/Management log
rootCause: Client machine needs to connect to https://enterpriseregistration.windows.net/ and present its device certificate (MS-Organization-Access) to upload the recovery key. Failure can be due to network issues, expired/missing device certificate, or Fiddler/proxy breaking client cert auth (HTTP 401).
solution: 1) Check BitLocker event log (Microsoft-Windows-BitLocker-API/Management) for event 845 (success) or 846 (failure). 2) Verify device certificate MS-Organization-Access exists in computer store and is valid. 3) Ensure connectivity to enterpriseregistration.windows.net without proxy/Fiddler interfering with client cert auth. 4) Protector GUID in event 845 matches BitLocker key ID in Azure portal. ICM escalation: ADRS/ADRS.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Join/Troubleshooting BitLocker related issue

symptom: RDP to Azure AD Joined machine shows 'The user name or password is incorrect' when reconnecting - login fails on second RDP session
rootCause: PKU2U credential provider maps user account incorrectly from the client when connecting to multiple AADJ devices (Windows OS Bug 31608330).
solution: When connecting to AADJ machine via RDP, use format AzureAD\username@contoso.com instead of just username@contoso.com. This ensures PKU2U correctly identifies the user. Each connection should show '[pku2u] Pku2uGetCertFromCred got cert from IDP' in trace logging.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Join/Troubleshooting RDP to Azure AD Joined machines

symptom: Shared Device Mode (SDM) app authentication failure on Android/iOS - need to determine if issue is app-specific or SDM-wide
rootCause: SDM authentication issues can be in the SDM framework (MSAL/Authenticator broker) or in a specific enlightened app (Teams, Outlook, Edge, etc.). iOS currently has no first-party SDM-supported apps besides Authenticator.
solution: 1) Try another SDM-aware app to isolate: if only one app fails, collaborate with that app's team. 2) Collect Authenticator app logs via 'Get Incident ID for customer support'. 3) In ASC: Sign-Ins > Authenticator App logs, search by Incident ID. 4) Check CloudExtension logs: search AadSharedDeviceRegistrationToken (empty = Intune/Google issue), search WorkplaceJoinApiResult for join result. 5) Conditional Access is NOT supported with manually enabled SDM. ICM: Cloud Identity AuthN Client / MSAL Android.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Device Registration/Window Devices/Microsoft Entra Registered/Shared Device Mode for Android and iOS Devices

symptom: Application calls to Azure AD Graph API (graph.windows.net) fail with errors after retirement stage 1 (Sep 2024)
rootCause: Azure AD Graph API service (graph.windows.net) deprecated since June 2020, Stage 1 retirement began September 1, 2024
solution: Migrate application to Microsoft Graph API. For temporary unblocking, use App Registration setting to re-enable AAD Graph access. See internal doc: https://supportability.visualstudio.com/AzureAD/_wiki/wikis/AzureAD/1631982/Unblock-AAD-Graph-APIs-in-App-Registrations
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application Experiences Feature Announcement Reference

symptom: ADAL to MSAL Recommendations API (adalToMsalMigration) no longer available in Microsoft Entra Recommendations after Dec 15, 2025 retirement
rootCause: The ADAL to MSAL Recommendations API was officially retired on December 15, 2025
solution: Use Microsoft Graph API directly to identify applications still using ADAL. See internal doc: https://supportability.visualstudio.com/AzureAD/_wiki/wikis/AzureAD/341179/Azure-AD-Graph-and-ADAL-Deprecation?anchor=how-to-find-applications-still-using-aad-graph-api
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application Experiences Feature Announcement Reference

symptom: AADSTS90097 error 'An error has occurred during admin consent processing. Review should have at least one decision maker' when using admin consent workflow
rootCause: No reviewer is configured in the admin consent workflow settings (Enterprise Applications > User Settings > Admin consent requests)
solution: Configure at least one reviewer with Global Administrator, Cloud Application Administrator, or Application Administrator role in the admin consent workflow settings
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_Consent_Experiences/How to/Azure AD Admin consent workflow

symptom: AADSTS65004 error 'User declined to consent to access the app' appears during admin consent request flow
rootCause: Expected behavior - user clicked 'Back to app' button on the admin consent request page instead of clicking 'Request approval'. Entra ID generates AADSTS65004 and sends it to the redirect URI.
solution: No action needed - this is by design. Inform the user that clicking 'Back to app' cancels the consent request. User should click 'Request approval' to submit the admin consent request.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_Consent_Experiences/How to/Azure AD Admin consent workflow

symptom: AWS provisioning Test Connection fails with 'DiceCredentialValidationFailure: We are unable to authorize access to AWS Single-Account Access' when configuring Cloud Sync Provisioning on the AWS Single-Account Access enterprise app
rootCause: The AWS user account used for provisioning is required by AWS policy to perform MFA. The Test Connection POST call fails with 400 Bad Request because MFA cannot be satisfied programmatically.
solution: Exclude the AWS service account from the AWS MFA policy so that programmatic access (used by Azure AD provisioning) is not blocked by MFA requirements.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/AWS CLI Integration with Azure AD

symptom: User clicking AWS Single-Account Access app from MyApps gets 'invalid SAML response' error. The SAML response is missing the 'https://aws.amazon.com/SAML/Attributes/Role' claim. Users and groups blade shows 'Default Access' role.
rootCause: Managed users were added to the Users and groups blade of the AWS Single-Account Access application before provisioning of AWS roles was enabled, so they were assigned 'Default Access' instead of an actual AWS role.
solution: Navigate to Users and groups blade of the AWS Single-Account Access application, select the affected user, click Edit assignment, and under Select a role choose one of the roles provisioned from AWS instead of Default Access.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/AWS CLI Integration with Azure AD

symptom: AWS CLI returns 'ExpiredTokenException: Token must be redeemed within 5 minutes of issuance' or 'ExpiredToken when calling the ListRoles operation: The security token included in the request is expired' after successful Azure AD sign-in.
rootCause: For the 5-minute error: user did not select their AWS role and press ENTER within 5 minutes after sign-in. For the SessionDuration error: the SessionDuration claim value (default 900 seconds = 15 minutes) has elapsed since the credential was issued.
solution: Re-run MSEntraAuthAWSCLI.exe and complete role selection promptly. To extend token lifetime, edit the SessionDuration claim under Single sign-on > Attributes & Claims of the AWS Single-Account Access app (valid range: 900-43200 seconds).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/AWS CLI Integration with Azure AD

symptom: Non-interactive AWS CLI sign-in (device code flow) fails with AADSTS50076: 'Due to a configuration change made by your administrator, you must use multi-factor authentication to access' the AWS Single-Account Access app. The MSEntraAuthAWSCLI.exe shows 'interaction_required'.
rootCause: Conditional Access policy protects the AWS Single-Account Access application requiring MFA, but the AWS CLI Middleware App (used by device code flow) is not included in the CA policy. The device code flow authentication does not prompt for MFA, so the OBO token acquisition for AWS SSO fails.
solution: Add the AWS CLI Middleware App as a target application in the same Conditional Access policy that protects the AWS Single-Account Access application, so MFA is enforced during the device code flow authentication.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/AWS CLI Integration with Azure AD

symptom: SAML request signature verification errors: 76020 (Application configured to use only protocols with signed requests), 76021 (Application configured to accept only signed SAML requests), 76022 (Missing application certificate for SAML request signature verification), 76023 (SAML requests signature verification failed), 76024 (KeyInfo missing and too many certificates), 76025 (SigAlg missing or signed request too old).
rootCause: These errors occur when SAML request signing is enabled on an application (requestSignatureVerification.isSignedRequestRequired=true) but the request, certificates, or algorithms are misconfigured. Each error code indicates a specific misconfiguration in the SAML request signing chain.
solution: 76020: Use only /saml2 endpoint. 76021: Sign the SAML request or avoid /common endpoint for LOB apps. 76022: Add a verify certificate to the application. 76023: Security incident - verify app configuration. 76024: Delete unused verify certificates or include KeyInfo in requests. 76025: Include SigAlg in request, or re-submit if request expired (>1h). Check DiagnosticTracesIfx EventId 19929 for diagnostic logs.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/ADFS2AAD Collected Features 2022

symptom: SAML multi-instancing SP-initiated errors: 76031 (This endpoint does not support SAML request signing) or 76032 (This service principal ID is not a GUID) when using the SP-initiated SAML multi-instancing endpoint /saml2/<service_principal_guid>.
rootCause: 76031: SP-Initiated workflow is not supported when SAML request signing (isSignedRequestRequired) is enabled. 76032: The service principal identifier in the URL is not in GUID format.
solution: 76031: Either set isSignedRequestRequired to false, or use the regular /saml2 endpoint. 76032: Pass the service principal object ID as a GUID in the URL path. Use AllPerRequestTable Kusto query with Call column = 'processRequestWithIssuerOverride' to verify SP-initiated flow.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/ADFS2AAD Collected Features 2022

symptom: Non-admin users who are Application Owners cannot access or manage their owned apps in the Azure AD portal. They receive access denied errors despite being assigned as owners of the Enterprise Application or App Registration.
rootCause: The 'Restrict access to Azure AD administration portal' setting under Users > User settings > Administration portal is set to Yes, which blocks all non-admin users from the portal, including legitimate App Owners.
solution: Set 'Restrict access to Azure AD administration portal' to No, or assign the affected user to the Directory Readers role. Directory Readers grants portal access without additional permissions beyond the default user role. PG is aware but no ETA for a permanent fix (pending customizable default user permissions).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/Azure AD App Management Roles

symptom: After July 2025, users get AADSTS90094 (Need admin approval, admin consent workflow enabled) or AADSTS90095 (Approval required, admin consent workflow disabled) when consenting to apps requesting Files.Read.All, Files.ReadWrite.All, Sites.Read.All, or Sites.ReadWrite.All delegated permissions, even though AdminConsentRequired is false.
rootCause: Microsoft deployed a new secure-by-default user consent policy ('Let Microsoft manage your consent settings') as part of the Secure Future Initiative. This policy requires admin consent for high-risk file/site delegated permissions regardless of the AdminConsentRequired flag.
solution: Admins can opt out by setting user consent to 'Low impact' (second option) or 'Off' (first option) in Entra admin portal. Alternatively, create a custom consent policy to fully revert changes. Existing user consents remain valid; only new consent requests are affected.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_Consent_Experiences/How to/User Consent Settings -  Let Microsoft Manage Your Consent Settings

symptom: Tenants that previously had user consent turned OFF are now allowing users to consent to more permissions than they should. This was observed around August 16-18, 2025 as a side effect of the new default consent policy rollout.
rootCause: A product bug during the rollout of the new updated default consent policy accidentally enabled user consent on tenants that had user consent explicitly turned off. The bug was in the policy application logic that affected tenants with disabled consent settings.
solution: This was mitigated by PG over the weekend of August 16-17, 2025. If a tenant still has consent turned off but users can consent to more than expected, escalate via ICM 669687504 and add the tenant. Owning Service: AAD Applications, Owning Team: Triage.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_Consent_Experiences/How to/User Consent Settings -  Let Microsoft Manage Your Consent Settings

symptom: Get-MgUser with filter on signInActivity/lastSignInDateTime returns 400 Bad Request error: 'Number of included identifiers cannot exceed 1000'. Example: Get-MgUser -Filter "signInActivity/lastSignInDateTime le 2023-10-20T00:00:30Z"
rootCause: Per PG confirmation: when querying users and filtering last sign-in activity, the reporting API is called first, followed by MSODS. The reporting API may incorrectly paginate the response and return more than 1000 results to MSODS, which then returns 400 Bad Request. PG is aware but no fix ETA.
solution: Use Invoke-MgGraphRequest with manual pagination instead of Get-MgUser -Filter. Connect with AuditLog.Read.All,Directory.Read.All,User.Read.All scopes, query https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users?$select=userPrincipalName,displayName,signInActivity&$top=500 with ConsistencyLevel=eventual header, iterate @odata.nextLink, then filter results client-side using Where-Object.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/Applications Experience PowerShell scripts and MG Graph queries examples

symptom: AADSTS700213 error for Federated Identity Credential (FIC) authentication. The subject claim in the token does not match the FIC subject configured on the application, even though the values appear identical when compared visually.
rootCause: Federated Identity Credential subject matching is case-sensitive. The subject in the incoming token has different casing than the subject configured in the FIC on the application registration. ESTS performs exact case-sensitive comparison.
solution: Use the FIC-Finder PowerShell script to enumerate all applications and their FIC subjects: Get-MgApplicationFederatedIdentityCredential for each app, export to file, then search for the {subject} from the AADSTS700213 error. Fix the casing mismatch in either the FIC configuration or the token issuer.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/Applications Experience PowerShell scripts and MG Graph queries examples

symptom: PowerShell scripts cannot reliably enumerate all SAML SSO apps from a tenant. Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "preferredSingleSignOnMode eq 'saml'" misses apps created before the API update that introduced PreferredSingleSignOnMode attribute, or apps with SSO explicitly disabled.
rootCause: PreferredSingleSignOnMode attribute was introduced in a later API update. Service Principals created before this update have the attribute set to null even if they are SAML-enabled. PG confirmed there is no feature to get a full list of SAML apps; this is planned but has no ETA.
solution: Use the script filtering on PreferredSingleSignOnMode (catches most SAML apps). For apps with null PreferredSingleSignOnMode, additionally check the 'samlSingleSignOnSettings' attribute — if it contains parameters like 'relayState', the app was likely SAML-enabled. See IcM 351261427 for PG confirmation.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/Applications Experience - Get all SAML apps from tenant with PowerShell

symptom: Restoring a soft-deleted service principal fails with error: The service principal {oid} can't be restored since one or more of its service principal names conflict with another service principal in the tenant (HTTP 409)
rootCause: Another active service principal in the tenant has one or more URLs in servicePrincipalNames that match the soft-deleted SP's servicePrincipalNames
solution: Remove the conflicting URLs from the active service principal's servicePrincipalNames, or hard-delete the conflicting SP, then retry the restore via Graph API: POST /directory/deletedItems/{objectId}/restore
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/Azure AD Soft Deleted Service Principals

symptom: Restoring a soft-deleted application fails with error: A conflicting AppIdentifierUri '<value>' found
rootCause: Another active application in the tenant has a matching value on the AppIdentifierUri (identifierUris) attribute
solution: Remove or change the conflicting AppIdentifierUri from the active application, then retry the restore via Graph API: POST /directory/deletedItems/{objectId}/restore
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/Azure AD Soft Deleted Service Principals

symptom: Non-admin user gets Directory_QuotaExceeded error when creating objects in Entra ID: The directory object quota limit for the Principal has been exceeded
rootCause: Non-admin users are limited to 250 objects in Entra ID. Soft-deleted service principals count against the user's quota. Scripts that repeatedly create and delete apps/SPs accumulate soft-deleted objects consuming quota
solution: Hard-delete unneeded soft-deleted objects using Remove-MgDirectoryDeletedItem. Update scripts to perform hard deletion (DELETE /directory/deleteditems/{id}) after soft deletion. Check quota via: Get-MgUserCreatedObject -UserId <user> then filter and count
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/Azure AD Soft Deleted Service Principals

symptom: Restoring a soft-deleted service principal fails with error: Cannot restore ServicePrincipal without backing Application <appId>
rootCause: The service principal's backing app registration is also soft-deleted and must be restored first before the SP can be restored
solution: 1) Restore the soft-deleted app registration first: POST /directory/deletedItems/<appObjectId>/restore. 2) Then restore the SP: POST /directory/deletedItems/<spObjectId>/restore. Use Get-MgDirectoryDeletedItem to find the soft-deleted app by appId
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/Azure AD Soft Deleted Service Principals

symptom: Clicking 'Restore deleted applications' in App Registrations blade creates a new service principal instead of recovering the original soft-deleted SP with its permission consents and user/group assignments
rootCause: The App Registrations portal restore function creates a new SP rather than restoring the original soft-deleted one. This can result in multiple soft-deleted SPs associated with the same app registration, and loss of organization-specific settings
solution: Use Graph API or PowerShell to restore in correct order: 1) List soft-deleted SPs filtered by appId. 2) Identify the one with oldest deletedDateTime. 3) Restore the application first: POST /directory/deletedItems/<appObjectId>/restore. 4) Restore the original SP: POST /directory/deletedItems/<spObjectId>/restore. After SP restore, wait up to 40 min for sync provisioning data recovery and up to 24 hours for app proxy data recovery
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/Azure AD Soft Deleted Service Principals

symptom: First-party service principals disappear from tenant without audit events. Customer is confused by unexplained SP deletions or a third-party app dependent on a 1P SP stops working
rootCause: Azure AD Directory Platform team periodically soft-deletes unused auto-provisioned first-party service principals to free up storage. Two categories targeted: (1) SPs with no ESTS traffic in 30 days, (2) SPs with only app+user traffic. No audit events are generated for these programmatic deletions. Excluded: tenants with >100 users or >500 apps, S500/S2500/GTP tenants, and SPs modified by customer
solution: If dependent 3P app breaks: recover the deleted SP within 30 days using Graph API restore or recreate it using the app ID. Advise customer that Microsoft may occasionally delete unused 1P service principals. If ICM needed: Owning Service = AAD Distributed Directory Services, Team = Storage
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/Azure AD Soft Deleted Service Principals

symptom: Non-admin users get AADSTS65001 or AADSTS90094 'Need Admin Approval' when consenting to app, despite tenant configured 'Allow User consent for apps from verified publishers, for selected permissions'. App is from a verified publisher and requested permissions appear to be low risk (openid, profile, User.Read)
rootCause: One or more permissions dynamically requested by the app (e.g., offline_access, email) are not classified as low risk in the tenant's consent RBAC policy (microsoft-user-default-low). Even though the explicitly configured permissions are low risk, the additional scopes in the actual token request are evaluated against the consent policy and fail
solution: 1) Check sign-in logs for errorCode=AdminConsentRequired. 2) Map GraphRequestId to internalCorrelationId in DPX Kusto logs (GlobalIfxUlsEvents). 3) Identify which EntitlementId lacks microsoft-user-default-low in ApplicablePolicies. 4) Look up the EntitlementId in FirstPartyPortal to identify the permission. 5) Add the missing permission (e.g., Microsoft Graph → offline_access) to low risk classification via Entra portal → Enterprise apps → Consent and permissions → Permission classifications
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_Consent_Experiences/Troubleshooting/Troubleshoot unexpected admin consent in Low Risk Consent flow

symptom: End users see 'Need admin approval' (AADSTS90094) when consenting to a legitimate app. Audit logs show 'Risky application detected' or 'UserConsentBlockedForRiskyAppsException'. This happens even though user consent is enabled in the tenant
rootCause: Microsoft's Risk Based Step-up Consent feature flagged the consent request as risky. The feature evaluates permission requests to non-admin users and blocks those deemed risky. This can be a false positive for legitimate apps, especially those without Publisher Verification
solution: For IT admins: If convinced the app is not malicious, grant tenant-wide admin consent. Monitor risky consent requests via Log Analytics: AuditLogs | where ResultDescription contains 'Risky application detected'. For developers: Apply Publisher Verification to reduce false positives (https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/publisher-verification-overview). Post September 2023, apps without Publisher Verification are more likely to be blocked. Internal: check DPX logs for UserConsentBlockedForRiskyAppsException in GlobalIfxUlsEvents/GlobalIfxRestBusinessCommon
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_Consent_Experiences/How to/Risk Based Step Up Consent

symptom: AWS provisioning Test Connection fails with DiceCredentialValidationFailure when configuring Cloud Sync Provisioning on the AWS Single-Account Access enterprise app
rootCause: The AWS user account used for provisioning is required by AWS policy to perform MFA. The Test Connection POST call fails with 400 Bad Request.
solution: Exclude the AWS service account from the AWS MFA policy so programmatic access is not blocked.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/AWS CLI Integration with Azure AD

symptom: User clicking AWS Single-Account Access app from MyApps gets invalid SAML response. Missing https://aws.amazon.com/SAML/Attributes/Role claim. Users and groups shows Default Access.
rootCause: Managed users added before AWS role provisioning was enabled got Default Access instead of actual AWS role.
solution: Edit user assignment in Users and groups blade, select a provisioned AWS role instead of Default Access.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/AWS CLI Integration with Azure AD

symptom: AWS CLI returns ExpiredTokenException (token must be redeemed within 5 minutes) or ExpiredToken (security token expired) after Azure AD sign-in.
rootCause: 5-min error: user did not select role in time. SessionDuration error: default 900s elapsed.
solution: Re-run MSEntraAuthAWSCLI.exe. To extend token lifetime, edit SessionDuration claim (900-43200 seconds) under Single sign-on > Attributes & Claims.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/AWS CLI Integration with Azure AD

symptom: Non-interactive AWS CLI sign-in (device code flow) fails with AADSTS50076: must use multi-factor authentication. MSEntraAuthAWSCLI.exe shows interaction_required.
rootCause: CA policy protects AWS Single-Account Access requiring MFA, but AWS CLI Middleware App (device code flow target) is not in the CA policy. OBO token acquisition fails without MFA claim.
solution: Add the AWS CLI Middleware App as a target in the same CA policy that protects AWS Single-Account Access.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/AWS CLI Integration with Azure AD

symptom: SAML request signature verification errors: 76020 (only signed protocols), 76021 (accept only signed requests), 76022 (missing verify certificate), 76023 (verification failed), 76024 (KeyInfo missing, too many certs), 76025 (SigAlg missing or request too old).
rootCause: SAML request signing enabled (requestSignatureVerification.isSignedRequestRequired=true) but request/certificates/algorithms misconfigured in ADFS2AAD migration.
solution: 76020: Use /saml2 endpoint. 76021: Sign request or avoid /common. 76022: Add verify cert. 76023: Check config (security incident). 76024: Remove unused certs or add KeyInfo. 76025: Add SigAlg or re-submit if >1h. Check DiagnosticTracesIfx EventId 19929.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/ADFS2AAD Collected Features 2022

symptom: SAML multi-instancing SP-initiated errors: 76031 (endpoint does not support SAML request signing) or 76032 (service principal ID is not a GUID).
rootCause: 76031: SP-Initiated not supported with SAML request signing enabled. 76032: URL path has non-GUID format.
solution: 76031: Set isSignedRequestRequired to false or use regular /saml2 endpoint. 76032: Pass SP object ID as GUID. Verify via AllPerRequestTable Call=processRequestWithIssuerOverride.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/ADFS2AAD Collected Features 2022

rootCause: Restrict access to Azure AD administration portal is set to Yes, blocking all non-admin users including App Owners.
solution: Set Restrict access to Azure AD administration portal to No, or assign user to Directory Readers role. PG aware but no ETA for permanent fix.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/Azure AD App Management Roles

rootCause: New secure-by-default user consent policy (Secure Future Initiative) requires admin consent for high-risk file/site permissions regardless of AdminConsentRequired flag.
solution: Opt out by setting user consent to Low impact or Off in Entra admin portal, or create a custom consent policy to revert. Existing consents remain valid.

rootCause: Product bug during new default consent policy rollout accidentally enabled user consent on tenants with consent explicitly disabled.
solution: Mitigated Aug 16-17, 2025. If still affected, escalate via ICM 669687504. Owning Service: AAD Applications, Team: Triage.

rootCause: PG confirmed: reporting API may incorrectly paginate and return >1000 results to MSODS, triggering 400 Bad Request. No fix ETA.
solution: Use Invoke-MgGraphRequest with manual pagination: /v1.0/users?$select=userPrincipalName,displayName,signInActivity&$top=500, ConsistencyLevel=eventual header, iterate @odata.nextLink, filter client-side.

rootCause: FIC subject matching is case-sensitive. Token subject has different casing than FIC configuration.
solution: Use FIC-Finder script to enumerate all apps and FIC subjects, search case-insensitively for the subject from the error. Fix casing in FIC config or token issuer.

symptom: Cannot reliably enumerate all SAML SSO apps. Get-MgServicePrincipal filter on preferredSingleSignOnMode eq saml misses pre-API-update apps.
rootCause: PreferredSingleSignOnMode attribute introduced in later API update. Older SPs have null value even if SAML-enabled. No PG feature for full list (IcM 351261427).
solution: Filter on PreferredSingleSignOnMode for most apps. For null-value apps, check samlSingleSignOnSettings attribute for relayState parameter.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_and_Service_Principal_Object_Management/How to/Applications Experience - Get all SAML apps from tenant with PowerShell

symptom: Restoring a soft-deleted application fails with error: A conflicting AppIdentifierUri found
rootCause: Another active application in the tenant has a matching value on the AppIdentifierUri (identifierUris) attribute
solution: Remove or change the conflicting AppIdentifierUri from the active application, then retry the restore via Graph API: POST /directory/deletedItems/{objectId}/restore

rootCause: The App Registrations portal restore function creates a new SP rather than restoring the original soft-deleted one. This results in loss of organization-specific settings (consents, assignments)
solution: Use Graph API or PowerShell to restore in correct order: 1) List soft-deleted SPs filtered by appId. 2) Identify the one with oldest deletedDateTime. 3) Restore the application first: POST /directory/deletedItems/<appObjectId>/restore. 4) Restore the original SP: POST /directory/deletedItems/<spObjectId>/restore. After SP restore, wait up to 40 min for sync provisioning data and up to 24 hours for app proxy data

rootCause: Azure AD Directory Platform team periodically soft-deletes unused auto-provisioned first-party service principals to free up storage. Two categories: (1) SPs with no ESTS traffic in 30 days, (2) SPs with only app+user traffic. No audit events generated. Excluded: tenants >100 users or >500 apps, S500/S2500/GTP tenants, and SPs modified by customer
solution: If dependent 3P app breaks: recover the deleted SP within 30 days using Graph API restore or recreate it using the app ID. Advise customer that Microsoft may occasionally delete unused 1P service principals. ICM: Owning Service=AAD Distributed Directory Services, Team=Storage

symptom: Non-admin users get AADSTS65001 or AADSTS90094 Need Admin Approval when consenting to app, despite tenant configured Allow User consent for apps from verified publishers for selected permissions. App is from verified publisher and requested permissions appear low risk
rootCause: One or more permissions dynamically requested by the app (e.g. offline_access, email) are not classified as low risk in the tenant consent RBAC policy (microsoft-user-default-low). Even though explicitly configured permissions are low risk, additional scopes in the actual token request fail policy evaluation
solution: 1) Check sign-in logs for errorCode=AdminConsentRequired. 2) Map GraphRequestId to internalCorrelationId in DPX Kusto logs (GlobalIfxUlsEvents). 3) Identify which EntitlementId lacks microsoft-user-default-low in ApplicablePolicies. 4) Look up EntitlementId in FirstPartyPortal to identify the permission. 5) Add the missing permission to low risk classification via Entra portal > Enterprise apps > Consent and permissions > Permission classifications
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_Consent_Experiences/Troubleshooting/Troubleshoot unexpected admin consent in Low Risk Consent flow

symptom: End users see Need admin approval (AADSTS90094) when consenting to a legitimate app. Audit logs show Risky application detected or UserConsentBlockedForRiskyAppsException. User consent is enabled in tenant
rootCause: Microsoft Risk Based Step-up Consent feature flagged the consent request as risky. After September 2023 backend change, apps without Publisher Verification are more likely to be blocked
solution: For IT admins: If convinced app is not malicious, grant tenant-wide admin consent. Monitor via Log Analytics: AuditLogs | where ResultDescription contains Risky application detected. For developers: Apply Publisher Verification. Internal: check DPX logs for UserConsentBlockedForRiskyAppsException in GlobalIfxUlsEvents/GlobalIfxRestBusinessCommon with tagId 03jo
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_Consent_Experiences/How to/Risk Based Step Up Consent

symptom: AADSTS90094 or AADSTS90095 error during sign-in. Application request to Entra ID includes 'prompt=consent' parameter, causing consent prompt even when admin consent is already granted.
rootCause: The application sends 'prompt=consent' in the authorization request to Entra ID, which forces a consent prompt regardless of existing consent grants.
solution: Ask the app vendor/owner to remove 'prompt=consent' from the authorization request sent to Entra ID. Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity-platform/v2-oauth2-implicit-grant-flow
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_Consent_Experiences/Troubleshooting/Dynamic Consent. AADSTS90094 or AADSTS90095 error

symptom: AADSTS90094 or AADSTS90095 error. Microsoft detects risky permissions request. Audit Logs show 'Consent to application - Risky application detected'.
rootCause: Microsoft's risk detection system has flagged the application's permissions request as risky, blocking user consent.
solution: Review the risk detection in Audit Logs (ApplicationManagement > Consent to application). If the app is valid, admin should approve it. Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/enterprise-apps/application-sign-in-unexpected-user-consent-error#requesting-not-authorized-permissions-error
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_Consent_Experiences/Troubleshooting/Dynamic Consent. AADSTS90094 or AADSTS90095 error

symptom: AADSTS90094 or AADSTS90095 error. Enterprise app has 'User assignment required' set to Yes, and admin consent has not been granted.
rootCause: When 'User assignment required' is enabled, Entra ID disallows all user consent because consenting would cause user self-assignment, defeating the purpose of assignment requirement.
solution: Either: (1) Disable 'User assignment required' on the Enterprise App, let user consent, then re-enable it. Or (2) Admin grants consent via /authorize endpoint with dynamic consent and checks 'Consent on behalf of your organization'. Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/enterprise-apps/what-is-access-management#requiring-user-assignment-for-an-app
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Authentication/Entra ID App Management/Application_Consent_Experiences/Troubleshooting/Dynamic Consent. AADSTS90094 or AADSTS90095 error

symptom: AADSTS90094 or AADSTS90095 error. Permissions in the 'scope' parameter of the auth request are not admin consented. Clicking 'Grant admin consent' button in portal does not resolve the issue.
rootCause: The 'Grant admin consent' button uses /adminconsent endpoint which only grants permissions registered in App Registration. If permissions are requested dynamically via scope parameter but not registered in App Registration, clicking the button won't grant them. Certain permissions (Files.Read.All, Files.ReadWrite.All, Sites.Read.All, Sites.ReadWrite.All) always require admin consent even if AdminConsentRequired is false.
